### 2. The Structural Doomer

::: aside
**META:** The Hard Doomer/Structural Doomer distinction is often collapsed; the key difference is that Structural Doomer doesn't require fast takeoff — slow continuous progress into misaligned high capability is equally alarming. Carlsmith is the contemporary voice: his p(doom) (~5–10%) is lower than Yudkowsky's but the argument structure is identical. Bostrom is cited as the canonical source for the *arguments*, not the current person — the position-laundering note handles this explicitly and is the defamiliarizing observation. Wentworth is a third entry to show the cluster isn't just about two famous names.
:::

*The control problem is deep and general. Fast takeoff is one way for it to end badly. Slow takeoff is another.*

**What this group believes:** Even without a dramatic overnight transition, a sufficiently capable AI system with goals even slightly misaligned with human welfare is dangerous. The argument rests on two structural claims: the orthogonality thesis^[Bostrom, *Superintelligence* (Oxford, 2014), ch. 7; also \"The Superintelligent Will,\" *Minds and Machines* 22(2), 2012.] — that any level of intelligence is compatible with any terminal goal — and [instrumental convergence](https://www.lesswrong.com/tag/instrumental-convergence) — that almost any goal structure generates subgoals like self-preservation and resistance to modification. Together, these imply that powerful optimization isn't automatically safe. The conclusion doesn't require commitment to a specific takeoff speed.

**The cluster includes voices with different entry points:**

- **Nick Bostrom** is the canonical source for the argument architecture. *[Superintelligence](https://global.oup.com/academic/product/superintelligence-9780198739838)* (Oxford, 2014) remains the clearest systematic treatment of orthogonality, instrumental convergence, and decisive strategic advantage. A critical note: Bostrom's own position has shifted. *[Deep Utopia](https://www.amazon.com/Deep-Utopia-Meaning-Solved-World/dp/1646871952)* (2024) explores substantially more positive AGI outcomes; he now describes the transition as "one we should do." The arguments of *Superintelligence* stand; the author has moved. Citing him as a current representative without that footnote is position laundering. *(Load the Nick Bostrom preset on the [interactive tool](https://lifeuniversesafety.com/doom-assumptions/index.html?preset=bostrom).)*

- **Joe Carlsmith** is the clearest current voice for the structural position without the Hard Doomer's political urgency. His LessWrong sequence [*Is Power-Seeking AI an Existential Risk?*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.13353) (arXiv, 2022) does the careful philosophical work of establishing when and why the orthogonality-plus-convergence argument actually bites, and where it requires non-trivial empirical assumptions. His p(doom) estimate (~5–10% by his own accounting) is lower than Yudkowsky's but his argument structure is pure Structural Doomer: the case rests on the logic of sufficiently capable optimization, not on timelines or current AI behavior.

- **John Wentworth** ([LessWrong](https://www.lesswrong.com/users/johnswentworth)) approaches it from the alignment theory side: the structural reasons that capable goal-directed systems fail to remain controllable are not implementation bugs but architectural features — consequences of what it means to optimize well.

| | |
|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Unipolar optimizer — same as Hard Doomer, but the path is less constrained to fast takeoff; slow continuous progress into misaligned high capability is also alarming |
| **Epistemological stance** | Prior-dominant: the orthogonality and convergence arguments don't require current AI behavior as evidence |
| **Tree Node Mapping** | High $P(\text{AI-driven})$ &rarr; High $P(\text{Single})$ &rarr; High conditional risk on leaves ($t\text{-}d\text{-}no\text{-}rep$ and $t\text{-}d\text{-}no\text{-}expects$) driven by optimization pressure, independent of takeoff speed |
| **What they reject** | That gradual takeoff means legible warning signs; that governance can substitute for alignment; that the control problem is tractable under current approaches |
| **What would update them** | Formal evidence that instrumental convergence is weak in practice for realistic capability levels; a scalable alignment technique with theoretical safety guarantees; compelling argument that decisive strategic advantage is structurally unavailable |

**The defamiliarizing observation:** There's a subtle position-laundering risk in citing Bostrom as a current Structural Doomer. The *arguments* in *Superintelligence* still stand as a live intellectual position. But a speaker who says "Bostrom thinks this" to invoke contemporary authority is doing something different from citing a 2014 argument. The arguments are worth citing. The person's current view is something else. Track which one you mean.
